nativeRange.habitat: The Blue and Gold Blenny is found in small groups among live corals on lagoons and sheltered coastal reefs, typically at depths of roughly 1 to 12 metres.

decorPreferences: scattered live rock with crevices for perching and hiding

diet.notes: Generally accepts a varied marine diet: vitamin-enriched frozen brine and mysis shrimp, finely chopped seafood, and quality marine flake or pellet foods. Combtooth blennies also benefit from grazing on natural film algae in a mature tank.

summary: The Blue and Gold Blenny is a small combtooth blenny from the Western Pacific, recognizable by its vivid blue head and yellow body. It is generally considered peaceful with most reef tankmates, though occasional nipping at SPS coral polyps has been reported.
careNotes: Two color forms are commonly seen in the trade: one with a yellow body and blue head, and a dusky form that is pale blue overall with a yellow dorsal and caudal fin — both share the diagnostic black anal spot that gives the species its scientific name (lividanalis). The Blue and Gold Blenny tends to spend much of its time hopping and perching out in the open between bouts of retreating into rockwork, so a scattered, crevice-rich aquascape is generally advised. Like most blennies, it has been known to jump from open tanks, so a tightly sealed lid is strongly recommended. It is considered reef-safe in most cases, but has been reported to occasionally nip at Acropora and other SPS coral polyps or bases; the risk tends to be higher in lightly stocked SPS displays. It tends to become territorial toward conspecifics and other members of the genus Ecsenius, so keeping one per tank is generally advised unless the system is very large.
breedingNotes: Oviparous with demersal, adhesive eggs in the wild; males are reported to become more colorful and territorial during the breeding season. Captive spawning has been documented sporadically — eggs are reported to hatch in roughly 3–4 days, with fry reared on rotifers and newly hatched brine shrimp, but rearing is not routinely successful in home aquaria.

additional.name: FishBase
additional.notes: Confirmed scientific name, family (Blenniidae), order (Blenniiformes), max length 5 cm TL, depth range 1–12 m, and Western Pacific distribution (Philippines, Indonesia, Northern Australia, PNG, Solomon Islands). FishBase wild temperature (28–29.3°C) runs warmer than typical captive hobby ranges; captive value averaged across LiveAquaria + tankfacts.

additional.name: Kraken Corals
additional.notes: Third opinion on minimum tank size — confirms 30 gallons (~120 L) as the practical minimum, supporting the cautious-larger pick over LiveAquaria's stated 10-gallon absolute minimum.

fish.breedingNotes: Pelagic-larva rearing in home aquaria is reported as challenging; eggs are demersal and adhesive but the rotifer/brine-shrimp rearing phase has a low survival rate without dedicated grow-out systems.
